did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a novel Fuzzy Multilayer Perceptron (F-MLP), a type of artificial neural network, to detect irregularity in skin lesion borders using dermoscopic images. It also employs a U-Net architecture (deep learning) for skin lesion segmentation.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled 'ORIGINAL RESEARCH' in the header. It presents a novel Fuzzy Multilayer Perceptron (F-MLP) algorithm, describes specific methods including U-Net segmentation and FuzzEdge border detection, reports experimental results with confusion matrices and accuracy metrics (e.g., 95.2% accuracy), and compares the proposed method against state-of-the-art classifiers. The structure includes Introduction, Related Work, Methods, Results, and Conclusion, which are characteristic of an original research study rather than a review.

title: A Novel Fuzzy Multilayer Perceptron (F-MLP) for the Detection of Irregularity in Skin Lesion Border Using Dermoscopic Images
brief_summary: This study addresses the detection of irregularity in skin lesion borders, a key feature in melanoma diagnosis, by proposing a novel Fuzzy Multilayer Perceptron (F-MLP). The methodology involves segmenting skin lesions from dermoscopic images using a U-Net architecture trained on the ISIC 2018 dataset, followed by border extraction and measurement of irregularity features. The F-MLP algorithm incorporates type-II fuzzy logic and a fuzzy gradient descent optimization function to handle input ambiguity during the classification of border irregularity. The model was evaluated against standard neural networks and other state-of-the-art classification methods using accuracy metrics derived from confusion matrices. The results indicate that the proposed F-MLP outperformed most existing methods, particularly its standard neural network counterpart, in detecting border irregularity. However, the study notes that the fuzzy neural network required more time for training compared to standard approaches.

lesion_types_studied.details: Melanoma is the primary disease context for the study, as border irregularity is a key diagnostic feature. The study uses the ISIC 2018 dataset which contains melanoma images, but the specific classification task performed by the F-MLP is binary (regular vs. irregular borders), not melanoma vs. non-melanoma.

Mentioned in the context of distinguishing melanoma from benign lesions and in related work citations (e.g., Jaworek-Korjakowska and Tadeusiewicz using naevi). The current study's dataset (ISIC 2018) includes benign lesions, but the F-MLP experiment focuses on border regularity rather than lesion type classification.
One of the two classes in the binary classification task of the F-MLP model, used to detect border irregularity. Table 4 shows 33 true positives for 'Regular'.
One of the two classes in the binary classification task of the F-MLP model, representing the irregularity feature in the ABCD rule. Table 4 shows 24 true positives for 'Irregular'.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesion border irregularity (Regular vs. Irregular)
all_task_types.details: The F-MLP model predicts a discrete diagnostic category for border irregularity. The output layer has 1 neuron representing the final classification result. Prediction probabilities are generated and thresholded to decide between 'regular' or 'irregular' borders. The task is evaluated using confusion matrices, accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018: Skin Lesion Analysis Toward Melanoma Detection
datasets_used.description: A public grand challenge dataset containing dermoscopy images. The study utilized a subset of this dataset for both training the U-Net segmentation model and testing it, and subsequently for the border irregularity classification task.

use.training: 1777 images were used to train the U-Net architecture for skin lesion segmentation.
use.testing: 158 images were used to test the U-Net segmentation performance (Dice similarity 83.8%). The segmented lesions from these 158 images were then used to extract border irregularity measures (fractal dimension and convexity) and label them as regular or irregular by a dermatologist for training and testing the F-MLP classifier.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the classification performance of the F-MLP and standard neural network models by calculating accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.
To compare the performance of the proposed F-MLP against state-of-the-art classification methods and its standard neural network counterpart.
To evaluate the diagnostic performance of the border irregularity detection method.
To visualize the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity for the border irregularity detection method (cited from related work Arbisala and Claridge with an AUC of 0.76).
To evaluate the performance of the U-Net segmentation model (reported as 83.8%).
To evaluate the performance of a related CNN and Gaussian naive Bayes ensemble approach cited in the literature review (reported as 96.1%).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and confusion matrices derived from a fixed test set, without statistical significance testing between models.

ISIC 2018 (Segmentation).notes: The U-Net architecture was trained on 1777 images and tested on 158 images from the ISIC 2018 dataset. Training took 27.6 minutes and testing took 25.9 seconds.

ISIC 2018 (Border Irregularity Classific.notes: Confusion matrix for F-MLP (lower sigmoid): TP=33, TN=26, FP=3, FN=0. Confusion matrix for Standard Neural Network (SGD): TP=33, TN=26, FP=3, FN=0. The F-MLP training time was 0.58s and testing time was 0.01s.
Training time was 0.8s and testing time was 0.07s.
Training time was 0.6s and testing time was 0.08s.
Training time was 0.7s and testing time was 0.08s.

architecture_used.main_model: U-Net (for segmentation) and Fuzzy Multilayer Perceptron (F-MLP) (for classification)
architecture_used.input_size: 256 x 256 pixels (for U-Net segmentation); 2 features (fractal dimension and convexity) for F-MLP

U-Net.layers: 23 convolutional layers total, no fully connected layers
components: Encoder path: Convolution blocks followed by max-pooling (feature channels doubled at each downsampling step)
Decoder path: Upsampling, convolution (feature channels halved), concatenation with cropped encoder feature maps, two 3x3 convolutions, two ReLU operations, one 2x2 max-pooling (stride 2)
Final layer: 1x1 convolution mapping 64 feature vectors to classes
Strategy: Overlap-tile strategy with input mirroring for context extrapolation

structure: Input layer: 2 neurons (number of input features)
activation_functions: Type-II fuzzy sigmoid activation function (lower sigmoid ϕL and upper sigmoid ϕU)
Standard sigmoid for standard neural network counterpart

F-MLP.hardware: Intel Core i7 processor, 2.2 GHz, 16 GB memory

classification_or_output_head.U-Net: 1x1 convolution mapping to desired number of classes
classification_or_output_head.F-MLP: Single neuron output representing final classification result (regular vs. irregular border), thresholded based on prediction probability

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing to 256 × 256 pixels for U-Net input
Input mirroring (context extrapolation) for U-Net overlap-tile strategy
Data augmentation (rotating and flipping horizontally and vertically) to address class imbalance in regular border samples
Conversion of color images to grayscale for border detection processing
FuzzEdge fuzzy filtering for skin lesion border extraction
Box-counting method for fractal dimension calculation

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to address class imbalance in regular border samples. The 5 regular bordered images were rotated and flipped horizontally and vertically to produce multiple versions. This increased the total number of training images to 310 (157 regular, 153 irregular).

conclusions: The proposed Fuzzy Multilayer Perceptron (F-MLP) outperformed most state-of-the-art classification methods and its standard neural network counterpart in detecting skin lesion border irregularity.
The F-MLP achieved an accuracy of 95.2% on the 80:20 training-testing split, compared to 90.3% for the upper sigmoid variant.
The proposed fuzzy neural network was more time-consuming during the training phase compared to its traditional neural network counterpart.
The study demonstrated that incorporating type-II fuzzy logic and a fuzzy gradient descent optimization function helps mitigate the effects of input ambiguity on the learning process.
The U-Net architecture successfully segmented skin lesions from dermoscopic images, providing the necessary input for border extraction and irregularity measurement.
important_remarks: The study focuses on a binary classification task (regular vs. irregular borders) rather than melanoma diagnosis, limiting direct clinical applicability for cancer detection.
The dataset is heavily imbalanced, with only 5 regular border samples in the test set compared to 153 irregular border samples, potentially inflating accuracy metrics.
Data augmentation (rotation and flipping) was applied exclusively to the regular border class to address imbalance, which may introduce bias or overfitting for that class.
The U-Net segmentation model was trained and tested on the same ISIC 2018 dataset without external validation, raising concerns about generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
The proposed Fuzzy Multilayer Perceptron (F-MLP) requires significantly more training time (e.g., 0.58s vs. 0.02s for standard NN in 80:20 split) compared to its standard neural network counterpart.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to validate the significance of performance differences between models.
The method relies on a specific border irregularity measure combining fractal dimension and convexity, which may not capture all clinically relevant aspects of border irregularity.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding the application to lower-quality images (e.g., mobile phone cameras) and multi-class problems, indicating current scope is restricted to high-quality dermoscopic images.
